Example:\\\\n\\\\nreturn ( ); ... const styles = StyleSheet.create({ contentContainer: { paddingVertical: 20 } });\\\\n```\\\\n4. Exactly these lines:\\\\nhttps://github.com/facebook/react-native/blob/db45c280e2f9702d27e1f4e321a8c554a4af6181/packages/react-native/Libraries/Components/ScrollView/ScrollView.d.ts#L610-L611\\\\nIt should output all text:\\\\n```\\\\n   *   return (\\\\n   *     \\\\n   *     \\\\n   *   );\\\\n```\\\\n\\\",\\\"user\\\":{\\\"login\\\":\\\"psnet\\\"},\\\"comments\\\":1,\\\"created_at\\\":\\\"2026-02-17T00:15:55Z\\\",\\\"updated_at\\\":\\\"2026-02-22T11:58:31Z\\\"},{\\\"id\\\":3944552640,\\\"number\\\":63143,\\\"state\\\":\\\"OPEN\\\",\\\"title\\\":\\\"Add switch to disable _var unused variable prevention\\\",\\\"body\\\":\\\"### 🔍 Search Terms\\\\n\\\\nleading underscore unused variable\\\\n\\\\n### ✅ Viability Checklist\\\\n\\\\n- [x] This wouldn\\\\u0026#39;t be a breaking change in existing TypeScript/JavaScript code\\\\n- [x] This wouldn\\\\u0026#39;t change the runtime behavior of existing JavaScript code\\\\n- [x] This could be implemented without emitting different JS based on the types of the expressions\\\\n- [x] This isn\\\\u0026#39;t a runtime feature (e.g. library functionality, non-ECMAScript syntax with JavaScript output, new syntax sugar for JS, etc.)\\\\n- [x] This isn\\\\u0026#39;t a request to add a new utility type: https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/wiki/No-New-Utility-Types\\\\n- [x] This feature would agree with the rest of our Design Goals: https://github.com/Microsoft/TypeScript/wiki/TypeScript-Design-Goals\\\\n\\\\n### ⭐ Suggestion\\\\n\\\\nI always knew that _prefixed variables are ignored, but only recently I learned that it not only makes the IDE ignore unused variables, but it prevents the TS engine itself from even reporting whether a variable is used/unused to the IDE. I personally believe that a language engine should not be concerned by this and instead report everything raw and let the IDE and tooling handle it however the user wants.\\\\n\\\\nWhat I want is to allow TS engine to compile without errors (if a variable is unused), but still report this information to the IDE and tooling.\\\\n\\\\nTherefore, to not break existing codebases, I propose a new tsconfig flag to disable this implicit behavior.\\\\n\\\\nWith this new flag enabled, the TS engine will report whether a variable is used / unused to the IDE even if the variable name is prefixed with `_`.\\\\n\\\\nThis is especially useful in situations when one tries to avoid shadowing variable names, which could unknowingly lead to triggering this implicit behavior. And I am pretty sure that there is a myriad of other use cases where the loss of used/unused information is unwanted and unexpected.\\\\n\\\\n```\\\\nconst table = ...\\\\n// later\\\\nfunction replaceTable(_table) { // \\\\u0026lt;- wrong, this variable is unused, but the IDE can\\\\u0026#39;t report this because of TS\\\\n  table = makeTable()\\\\n}\\\\n```\\\\n\\\\nThis setting does not affect `noUnusedParameters`, instead, they will work in tandem.\\\\n\\\\n```\\\\n// tsconfig\\\\nnoUnusedParameters: false,\\\\nreportUnusedUnderscoreVariables: true // \\\\u0026lt;- just an adhoc placeholder\\\\n```\\\\n\\\\nThis will\\\\n- allow `tsc` to compile even if there are unused variables\\\\n- report unused variables prefixed with `_` as unused to the IDE\\\\n- show squiggles \\\\u0026#34;unused variable\\\\u0026#34; in the IDE\\\\n- allow user to configure their tooling (eslint, etc.) to use regular expressions to selectively ignore their chosen variation and be not locked to using `_name`\\\\n\\\\nrelated: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/79884683/what-is-causing-vs-code-to-ignore-prefixed-arguments-like-arg-ts6133\\\\n\\\\n### 📃 Motivating Example\\\\n\\\\nhttps://stackoverflow.com/questions/79884683/what-is-causing-vs-code-to-ignore-prefixed-arguments-like-arg-ts6133\\\\n\\\\nI wrote a function that uses a `column` variable, but there was a `column` defined in the scope, so I renamed it to `_column`. Later, I encountered a bug and while investigating, I found that the variable was not used, but this fact was unknown to the IDE.\\\\n\\\\nI have some unused parameters in other places, completely intentional, so I already disabled `noUnusedParameters` in tsconfig, because I don\\\\u0026#39;t use underscore `_name` to denote that a variable is unused. It\\\\u0026#39;s noise as I have IDE for that already.\\\\n\\\\n### 💻 Use Cases\\\\n\\\\n1. What do you want to use this for?\\\\n\\\\nI want to use variable names with underscore prefix to avoid shadowing existing variables, but I still want the IDE to report these variables to me for my consideration without breaking the build.\\\\n\\\\n2. What shortcomings exist with current approaches?\\\\n\\\\nRegardless of the `noUnusedParameters` setting, the IDE will not receive information whether a variable is used/unused if it is prefixed with underscore.\\\\n\\\\n3. What workarounds are you using in the meantime?\\\\n\\\\nThere is no workaround.\\\",\\\"user\\\":{\\\"login\\\":\\\"ackvf\\\"},\\\"comments\\\":2,\\\"created_at\\\":\\\"2026-02-15T17:36:37Z\\\",\\\"updated_at\\\":\\\"2026-02-22T11:58:32Z\\\"},{\\\"id\\\":3954067998,\\\"number\\\":63150,\\\"state\\\":\\\"OPEN\\\",\\\"title\\\":\\\"[JavaScript/TypeScript Language Hints] Hide default `Object` properties/methods until manually typed just like in Rust.\\\",\\\"body\\\":\\\"\\\\n\\\\n\\\\n\\\\n\\\\n\\\\nRight now if I create a class with static methods/properties or via namespace, it\\\\u0026#39;s very hard to distinguish between default and custom ones visually.\\\\n\\\\n\\\\u003cimg width=\\\\\\\"40%\\\\\\\" alt=\\\\\\\"Image\\\\\\\" src=\\\\\\\"https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fe83b90f-b946-445d-b8c0-ed220647b1ab\\\\\\\"/\\\\u003e\\\\n\\\\nI propose to hide those **default** methods, they are not useful to know for beginners, while for experienced ones, it\\\\u0026#39;s noisy. Leaving only defined in classes/namespaces.\\\\nI\\\\u0026#39;m looking from Rust lang analyzer extension perspective, which does exactly that, and I feel like it\\\\u0026#39;s much more convenient, especially if there will be more properties/methods in future.\\\\nIf the property is fully typed correctly, e.g. `Test.call`, the hint will appear just like typing `Test.constructor`, it\\\\u0026#39;s not appearing, but when typed - the hint appears with `Function`.\\\\n\\\\nThis can be an option to enable/disable.\\\",\\\"user\\\":{\\\"login\\\":\\\"FrameMuse\\\"},\\\"comments\\\":1,\\\"created_at\\\":\\\"2026-02-15T10:33:24Z\\\",\\\"updated_at\\\":\\\"2026-02-22T11:58:33Z\\\"},{\\\"id\\\":3933955999,\\\"number\\\":63132,\\\"state\\\":\\\"OPEN\\\",\\\"title\\\":\\\"Regression: Mapped types are no longer homomorphic when wrapped in certain conditional types\\\",\\\"body\\\":\\\"### 🔎 Search Terms\\\\n\\\\n\\\\u0026#34;homomorphic\\\\u0026#34; \\\\u0026#34;keyof\\\\u0026#34; \\\\u0026#34;extends\\\\u0026#34;\\\\n\\\\n### 🕗 Version \\\\u0026amp; Regression Information\\\\n\\\\n- This changed between versions 3.8.3 and 3.9.7\\\\n\\\\n### ⏯ Playground Link\\\\n\\\\nhttps://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?#code/C4TwDgpgBA0hIFkCGYA8AVAfFAvFA1vAPYBmU6UEAHsBAHYAmAzlAAoBORk7ocIUAfigBvKAG0YUAJZ0CxMugC6ALlhQAvlFV0IANwjsA3AChjoSFAAauWPGRo6AVwC2AIwOZDQA\\\\n\\\\n### 💻 Code\\\\n\\\\n```ts\\\\ntype KeyMap = keyof T extends PropertyKey ? { [K in keyof T]: K } : never;\\\\n\\\\ntype X = KeyMap;\\\\n```\\\\n\\\\n### 🙁 Actual behavior\\\\n\\\\n`X` is evaluated to\\\\n\\\\n```ts\\\\ntype X = {\\\\n    toString: \\\\u0026#34;toString\\\\u0026#34;;\\\\n    toFixed: \\\\u0026#34;toFixed\\\\u0026#34;;\\\\n    toExponential: \\\\u0026#34;toExponential\\\\u0026#34;;\\\\n    toPrecision: \\\\u0026#34;toPrecision\\\\u0026#34;;\\\\n    valueOf: \\\\u0026#34;valueOf\\\\u0026#34;;\\\\n    toLocaleString: \\\\u0026#34;toLocaleString\\\\u0026#34;;\\\\n}\\\\n```\\\\n\\\\n### 🙂 Expected behavior\\\\n\\\\n`X` should be evaluated to `number` because according to [the FAQ](https://github.com/Microsoft/TypeScript/wiki/FAQ#common-bugs-that-arent-bugs),\\\\n\\\\n\\\\u0026gt; Mapped types declared as `{ [ K in keyof T ]: U }` where T is a type parameter are known as `homomorphic mapped types`, which means that the mapped type is a structure preserving function of `T`. When type parameter `T` is instantiated with a primitive type the mapped type evaluates to the same primitive.\\\\n\\\\n### Additional information about the issue\\\\n\\\\nReplacing `PropertyKey` with Specifically the field\\\\u0026#39;s syntax and how it affects evaluation, which together the title phrases as resolution.\\\\n\\\\n@RyanCavanaugh mentioned such issues can be opened directly in the TypeScript repo [(see comment)](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/62753#issuecomment-3534104209).\\\\n\\\\nI no longer think that updates to `extends` docs wouldn\\\\u0026#39;t benefit the reader (as stated once in a [comment](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/62753#issuecomment-3536738103))\\\\n\\\\nI think mentioning Node.js style resolution for the config `extends` field, without clearly explaining how specifiers are resolved leads to misunderstandings of what to expect when using specifiers, starting with recognizing the diff between a path and a specifier.\\\\n\\\\nReasons being:\\\\n\\\\n- @RyanCavanaugh already mentioned `extends` docs to be outdated in another [comment](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/62753#issuecomment-3529443711)\\\\n-  #62915 mentions further updates to `extends`\\\\n-  other tools are considering updates to their implementation to adapt to new features of engines that involve support for `extends` and would probably benefit from up to date documentation ([see esbuild issue 4360](https://github.com/evanw/esbuild/issues/4360))\\\\n- previous attempts to align `extends` resolution in tools building on top of TypeScript tackled resolution according to Node.js, rather than using TS config resolution (the latter being undocumented or outdated and potentially misleading), as can be seen in this [VSC pull request](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/282969) being an attempt to close [a related issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/276592), in which in turn it is mentioned at least once that resolution is not the same and that the docs are unclear or outdated in this regard; also mentioning some of the differences and that resolution should follow compiler behaviour, not some engine unrelated to compiler configs.\\\\n \\\\nI\\\\u0026#39;ve copied the list of differences I am aware of from one of the linked issues down here below, since they are directly relevant to this issue:\\\\n\\\\n- ts normalizes paths differently than node does (e.g. ts normalizes `\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\` path separators [even in specifiers], node doesn\\\\u0026#39;t).\\\\n- node supports some protocols that tsc doesn\\\\u0026#39;t support for `extends` (e.g. in node you can import `node:`, `data:` and `file://`).\\\\n- Node has file extension restrictions, tsconfig has not.\\\\n- In node only specifiers starting with a dot indicate relative imports, while in tsconfig everything is relative unless it starts with at (@) or hash (#) or syntax indicating a root (I\\\\u0026#39;m only aware of path separators, but maybe there\\\\u0026#39;s logic to handle drive letters?) [Edit: this seems not, or no longer, to be the case for `extends`].\\\\n\\\\nApart from those syntactic differences, it would be worth noting that tsc is largely unaware of packages (support of package/module specifier syntax suggests otherwise).\\\\nThis has noteworthy implications for monorepos, project boundaries and relative paths in config files. E.g. one could test and answer the following question, but it would be nice if it were documented:\\\\n\\\\nWhat happens if I extend the config of another package from within a monorepo which has its own `files` or `includes`, without explicitly overriding `files` or `includes`? Will the current (implicit) defaults override the inherited or do I have to be explicit to override inherited entries? It must be the latter, otherwise it would never inherit. For monorepos this means one has to be careful not to include files from other packages (or the opposite, based on what you think would be intuitive), because resolved paths don\\\\u0026#39;t resolve to a `node_modules` subpath (that\\\\u0026#39;s a guess and may or may not depend on how symlinks are resolved, among other factors). The consequence would be inclusion of other package sources, as well as rendering `skipLibCheck` ineffective.\\\\n\\\\nEdit: Note, when using references and imports in modules, which the previous example did not imply, one gets mixed results according to #62880. Just mentioning this because it would be even more against intuition, if it turns out that references resolve differently from configs. In that other issue the hash-specifiers seem to match what was described before, while the at-specifiers seem to \\\\u0026#34;know\\\\u0026#34; about `node_modules`.\\\\n\\\\nAlso not clear from the docs on extends: Does `files` or `includes` override all inherited entries, or only the corresponding field? Following the links for docs on those fields it is not clear how those interact or interfere, neither per config file, nor when it comes to inheritance. Same for `exclude`. Does it affect `include` on a per file basis, or is it inherited as a field that will affect inheriting `includes`? A naive guess would be, fields are inherited independently, but that info is missing.\\\\n\\\\nSearch phrase I used to avoid opening a duplicate: \\\\u0026#34;update docs tsconfig extends\\\\u0026#34;\\\\n\\\\n- this is similar to #62753;
